styles.label: 略語/フルネーム 混在

ui_pattern_hints.hint: This is a numeric keypad layout. Use button position (row/col) and size (ratio) to assign labels following the standard numpad arrangement. Standard layout from top to bottom: Row 1: function keys (CLEAR, =, /, *). Row 2: 7, 8, 9, minus(-). Row 3: 4, 5, 6, plus(+). Row 4: 1, 2, 3, ENTER(tall/wide). Row 5: 0(wide), decimal(.), ENTER(continued). Wide buttons (ratio>=2.0) are likely the 0 key. Tall buttons (ratio<=0.6) are likely the ENTER key. Fill any remaining buttons with plausible function keys such as DEL, ESC, TAB, or SF/military equivalents.
This appears to be a phone keypad layout. Assign digits 0-9, *, # based on position. Include CALL, END, HOLD, MUTE, or similar telecom function keys for remaining buttons.

This appears to be a directional pad layout. Assign UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT to edge buttons and CENTER or OK/ENTER to the center button. Fill remaining buttons with CANCEL, BACK, MENU, SELECT as appropriate.
This appears to be a volume or mixer control layout. Assign VOL+, VOL-, MUTE, CH+, CH-, or level indicators. Use SF/military equivalents like GAIN, ATTENUATE, SIGNAL, LEVEL for remaining buttons.
This appears to be a menu or tab navigation layout. Assign section names relevant to the scene context, ensuring buttons in the same row or column share a logical category.

This is an application menu bar or toolbar. Generate menu item names that feel authentic to the application type suggested by the scene and keywords. Do NOT default to generic names like SYSTEM or FILE. Instead, invent contextually appropriate, varied menu names that fit the specific domain. For military/tactical apps: think about what a real operator would need quick access to. For medical apps: think about clinical workflows. For creative apps: think about creative tools and workflows. Each run should produce a different, plausible set of menu items. Keep labels concise (1-2 words), uppercase if SF/military context.

This is a tool palette or toolbox layout. Assign tool names as short labels. Common tool names: SELECT, MOVE, PEN, BRUSH, ERASER, CROP, ZOOM, FILL, TEXT, SHAPE, ROTATE, SCALE, SLICE, EYEDROP. For SF/military context use equivalents: SCAN, TARGET, MARK, TRACE, ERASE, ZOOM, LOCK, TAG, LABEL, PLOT, AIM, MEASURE. Tall or wide buttons may represent grouped tools.

This is a status display panel with multiple gauge or meter labels. Each button likely represents a system status indicator. Assign concise measurement or state names: TEMP, POWER, FUEL, SPEED, ALT, HEADING, SIGNAL, PRESSURE, VOLTAGE, CURRENT, LOAD, RANGE, DEPTH, RPM. For SF/military context: SYS STAT, PWR LVL, SIG STR, THREAT LVL, UNIT POS, FUEL CHK, ATMO, RADAR. Labels should be 1-3 words max to fit within the button.

This is a physical-style control panel with switches or buttons. Use toggle-style or action labels that feel tactile and mechanical: ARM/SAFE, ON/OFF, LOCK/REL, OPEN/CLOSE, ENGAGE, STANDBY, ACTIVE, BYPASS, OVERRIDE, HOLD, ABORT, LAUNCH, EJECT, PURGE, VENT, SEAL. Pair opposing functions on adjacent buttons where possible. Labels should feel like real hardware controls.

This is an aircraft cockpit or avionics panel. Use authentic aviation terminology. Instruments: ALT (Altimeter), ASI (Airspeed), VSI (Vertical Speed), HSI (Heading), ADI (Attitude Director), NAV, COM, ADF, VOR, ILS, DME, TCAS, GPWS. Controls: FLAP, GEAR, TRIM, THRUST, APU, HYD, ELEC, FUEL, PRESS, ENG1, ENG2, AUTOPILOT, FMS, MFD, PFD. Emergency: MAYDAY, XPDR, EMER, FIRE. Keep labels short and uppercase.

This is a ship bridge or naval operations panel. Use maritime/naval terminology. Navigation: HELM, RUDDER, THRUSTER, SPEED, HEADING, RADAR, SONAR, ECHO, GPS, LORAN, AIS. Engine: AHEAD, ASTERN, STOP, SLOW, HALF, FULL, TELEGRAPH. Systems: COMMS, BALLAST, BILGE, ANCHOR, MOORING, FOGHORN, SIGNAL. Military naval: FIRE CTRL, TORPEDO, COUNTERMSR, DECOY, EW, SONAR, PERISCOPE. Adjust based on vessel type (cargo, warship, submarine).

This is a submarine operations panel. Use submarine-specific terminology. Navigation: PERISCOPE, DEPTH, HEADING, TRIM, BALLAST, DIVE, SURFACE, SPEED. Systems: SONAR, HYDROPHONE, TORPEDO, COUNTERMSR, DECOY, COMMS, EW, REACTOR, OXYGEN, CO2, PRESSURE. Emergency: BLOW TANKS, EMERGENCY SURFACE, FLOOD, SEAL HATCH, ABANDON. Tactical: SILENT RUN, ACTIVE SONAR, PING, TRACK, FIRE CTRL. Keep labels concise and uppercase.

CRITICAL LAYOUT RULE - TWO COLUMN PARAMETER LIST: This layout has EXACTLY 2 columns. You MUST follow these rules strictly. col=1 buttons: ONLY parameter name labels. Short uppercase abbreviations. Max 10 chars. Examples for col=1: TEMP, VOLT, RPM, GYRO, SERVO, CPU, MEM, SIGNAL, FUEL, ARMOR, SPEED, TORQUE. col=2 buttons: ONLY value strings with units. NEVER put parameter names here. Max 10 chars. Examples for col=2: 98.6 C, 12.4 V, 4200rpm, 87 %, NOMINAL, ACTIVE, 0.8 kN, 64 MB, ONLINE. RULE: Each row is a pair. col=1 is the label, col=2 is its value. Match the value unit to the parameter in the same row (e.g. TEMP row -> temperature value, VOLT row -> voltage value). Do NOT generate parameter names for col=2. Do NOT generate values for col=1.

This is a robot or mech unit status panel. Use robotics and military hardware terminology. For parameter names: SERVO, GYRO, ACCEL, TORQUE, JOINT, TEMP, VOLT, AMP, PWR, CPU, MEM, COMMS, SENSOR, CAMERA, RADAR, WEAPON, SHIELD, ARMOR, MOBILITY, BALANCE. For values: include realistic units like deg/s, Nm, C, V, A, W, %, rpm, m/s, kN. Status values: NOMINAL, ACTIVE, STANDBY, FAULT, OFFLINE, LOCKED, ARMED, SAFE.
